agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: City-wide records retention schedule update (excluding police)
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will vote on a resolution to adopt updated city-wide records retention and destruction schedules, repealing the 1999 schedule. The new schedules set specific retention periods for all city records (excluding police) and authorize destruction after the period expires with written consent from the department head, city clerk, and city attorney. Updates to the schedule can be made without further council action with consent of the department head, city clerk, city attorney, and city manager. agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Blue Envelope Program for residents with disabilities
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider directing staff to create and implement a Blue Envelope Program for Richmond residents. The program is designed to help drivers with autism, dementia, anxiety, deafness, cerebral palsy, epilepsy, or other intellectual/developmental disabilities during police traffic stops. A blue envelope would hold the driver's licence, registration, and insurance, and signal to officers that the driver may need extra time or a different way to communicate. If approved, the city would also run a campaign to train police and first responders on how the program works. Staff would report back to council once the program is running. The program is voluntary, free, and self-implemented. Materials (envelopes, brochures, lanyards, decals, seatbelt covers, keychains, bracelets, stickers) would be available at the Police Department, ROCK vans, and other designated locations.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond council to vote on adding caste to anti-discrimination rules
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a resolution that would add caste as a protected category under the city's Human Rights and Human Relations Commission rules. If approved, the City Attorney's Office would be directed to amend Richmond Municipal Code Chapter 3.58.060(a)(3) to include caste-based discrimination and return to council for approval within 45 days. The resolution also empowers the Commission to conduct training programs for city employees, law enforcement, and community leaders, and to establish reporting and enforcement mechanisms for caste-based discrimination complaints. Costs would be covered by existing FY 2025-2026 diversity, equity, and inclusion program budgets.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if passed, this could affect your workplace policies, hiring practices, and require staff training on caste equity.

agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a proposed ordinance to rezone the John Haley Studio/House at 771 Ocean Avenue, Richmond, CA. agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider renewing Richmond Fund for Children and Youth
agenda_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council will hear a presentation on Article 15 of the City Charter, which governs the Richmond Fund for Children and Youth. Council will then discuss and provide direction on whether to renew the fund and what the renewal options should be. This is a proposed decision — nothing has been approved yet. The fund supports programs and services for children and youth in Richmond. The fund currently receives an annual allocation equal to 3% of unrestricted General Fund revenues, totaling approximately $99 million over a ten-year period (FY 2028-29 through FY 2037-38). Council must act by December 31, 2027 to extend the fund for an additional ten-year term by majority vote.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond to adopt 2025 California Fire Code with local changes
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will hold a public hearing and consider a proposed bylaw to adopt the 2025 California Fire Code and the 2025 California Wildland-Urban Interface Code, with local amendments. The state codes took effect January 1, 2026. Local amendments address Richmond-specific risks: high summer temperatures (up to 103°F), seismic faults (Hayward, San Andreas, Calaveras), narrow winding streets, and industrial sites. The new Wildland-Urban Interface Code consolidates wildfire provisions from multiple codes into Part 7 of Title 24. Appendix chapters adopted include B, C, D, F, H, I, J, K, and P (replacing previous appendices A–K).
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the public hearing on June 10, 2025 to voice support or concerns about the proposed fire code changes.

key_dates.label: Public hearing — proposed 2025 California Fire Code adoption (Richmond)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Richmond Council to consider report on protecting residents from ICE raids
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will receive an initial report on the City's ongoing and planned efforts to safeguard residents from federal U.S. immigration enforcement actions, including ICE raids. The report details existing protections under Ordinance 08-25 (RMC Chapter 2.30), which limits use of City resources for immigration enforcement, restricts collection/disclosure of immigration status, and limits compliance with civil detainers absent a judicial warrant. A Limited Assistance Protocol with signage and QR-code guidance for employees is in place. Staff will propose amendments to RMC Chapter 2.30 in late March/early April 2026 to further strengthen limitations. A draft Safeguard Richmond Coordinated Response Plan and community communication plan are being developed, with community meetings to be held for feedback.

key_dates.label: Proposed RMC Chapter 2.30 amendments — late March/early April 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Cheese Park upgrades — restrooms, playground, fencing, bike garden
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a resolution to direct staff to create a work plan for Cheese Park (La Moine Park) improvements, including permanent ADA-accessible restrooms, a drinking fountain with bottle-filler, playground restoration, safety fencing around the toddler area (addressing wildlife concerns including a mountain lion sighting), completion of volunteer-led repainting with durable materials, design of sports courts, senior/teen spaces and exercise equipment, and drainage fixes for the soccer field. Staff must identify funding sources in the FY 2026–27 Capital Improvement Program and operating budget. This is a proposal — not yet approved.

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved Blue Envelope Program for Richmond residents
minutes_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council has directed the city manager to create and implement a Blue Envelope Program for residents. The program is designed to help people with autism, dementia, or other cognitive conditions communicate more easily with police and first responders during traffic stops or emergencies. A promotional campaign will also be launched to inform police officers and first responders about the new program and how to participate. Once the program is up and running, staff will provide a presentation to council. This decision was finalized at the council meeting after being continued from earlier meetings in March 2026.
minutes_briefs.source_body:

This item was continued from the March 3, and March 17, 2026, meetings.

minutes_briefs.title: Richmond council adopted resolution opposing U.S. war on Iran
minutes_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council adopted a resolution opposing the U.S. war on Iran. The resolution condemns attacks by the United States and Israel on civilians and civilian infrastructure in Iran. It calls for immediate de-escalation and diplomacy, urges the U.S. Congress to use its constitutional war powers, and calls for an arms embargo on Israel. This is a formal statement of council's position; it does not create new local laws or regulations for businesses.
minutes_briefs.source_body:
N.3.b Stop Iran War Resolution
ADOPT a resolution opposing the U.S. war on Iran, condemning attacks by the United States and Israel on civilians and civilian infrastructure in Iran, calling for immediate de-escalation and diplomacy, urging Congress to exercise its constitutional war powers, and calling for an arms embargo on Israel - Councilmember Soheila Bana (510-672-3490).
Adopted Resolution No. 35-26

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved adding caste as a protected category in Richmond's human rights code
minutes_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council adopted a resolution to add caste as a protected category under the city's Municipal Code, making caste-based discrimination illegal in housing, employment, and public services. The City Attorney's Office must return with the final bylaw language within 45 days for council approval. This means businesses and landlords in Richmond cannot discriminate based on caste, and the Human Rights and Human Relations Commission will handle complaints.
minutes_briefs.action_required: Review your workplace and housing policies to ensure they prohibit caste-based discrimination before the bylaw takes effect.

key_dates.label: City Attorney returns with final bylaw language for council approval

minutes_briefs.industries: Labour & Employment, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Professional Services
minutes_briefs.source_body:

ADOPT a resolution that establishes a commitment to caste equity and the prevention of caste-based discrimination and DIRECT the City Attorney’s Office to add caste as a protected category under Richmond Municipal Code Chapter 3.58.060(a)(3) to be included within the purview of the City’s Human Rights and Human Relations Commission, and RETURN to City Council for approval within 45 days - Councilmembers Soheila Bana (510-672-3490), Jamelia Brown (510-412-2050), and Sue Wilson (510-609-2111).
Adopted Resolution No. 36-26

minutes_briefs.title: Council approved renaming Cesar Chavez Day to Farm Workers Movement Day
minutes_briefs.summary: Richmond City Council voted 6 to 1 to adopt a resolution changing the city's official holiday schedule. The holiday formerly known as Cesar Chavez Day will now be called Farm Workers Movement Day. Council also directed staff to bring a future agenda item to discuss sexual harassment, sexual abuse, and sexual assault, and the urgent need to protect and support survivors.
